title: Electricity
summary: Set up before or immediately after moving in. Can take 1-2 business days to activate.
steps: Call the OEPC English hotline or visit an OEPC office in person.
Provide your new address, move-in date, and passport or ID.
Request service start — give at least 1 business day notice.
A technician may need access to your breaker box. Be home if requested.
Pay your bill monthly at a convenience store (7-Eleven, Family Mart, Lawson) using the slip mailed to you, or set up auto-pay.
tips: Bring your residence card (在留カード) or passport to any in-person visit.
Convenience store bill payment is the easiest option for most families.
If your power won't turn on after setup, check the breaker box first — it may just be tripped.

title: Water
summary: Water is managed by the prefecture. Setup is straightforward but must be done in person or by phone.

contacts.label: Waterworks Bureau
contacts.note: Limited English. Have your address ready. Mon–Fri 8AM–5PM.

steps: Call the Waterworks Bureau to register your address and request service start.
Provide your move-in date and new address.
Water is usually active within 1 business day.
Bills are mailed bi-monthly and can be paid at any convenience store.
tips: If you're in base housing, water may already be handled — confirm with your housing office.
Off-base renters almost always need to set this up themselves.
Water quality in Okinawa is safe to drink from the tap.

title: Gas
summary: Most Okinawa homes use propane tanks, not city gas. Your landlord or property manager typically handles setup.

contacts.label: Ask your landlord or realtor
contacts.note: Gas provider varies by property. Your landlord will connect you with the right company.

steps: Ask your landlord or property manager which LP gas company services your building.
The gas company will schedule a setup appointment — you must be present.
A technician will inspect lines, connect the tank, and show you the shutoff valve.
Never attempt to connect or adjust gas lines yourself.
tips: Some apartments include gas in the rent — confirm with your landlord.
If you smell gas, leave immediately and call 119.
Keep the gas company's number saved — you'll need it if service is interrupted.

title: Internet
provider: Multiple providers available — setup takes 2–4 weeks
summary: Home internet in Japan requires a technician visit for installation. Start this process on day one — the wait catches most families off guard. You will be given a temporary solution in the meantime.

contacts.label: Ask your housing agency
contacts.note: Many housing agencies have preferred ISP partners and can recommend or coordinate setup for you.

steps: Contact your housing agency on move-in day and ask which internet providers serve your area.
Choose a provider and schedule a technician installation visit — expect 2–4 weeks wait time.
Most providers will loan you a Pocket WiFi device at signup to use while you wait for installation.
On installation day, be home — the technician needs access to your unit.
Bills are typically paid monthly by bank transfer or convenience store payment slip.
tips: Start this process immediately — do not wait until you feel settled.
Pocket WiFi loaned by your ISP is a standard bridge solution. Ask about it at signup.
Your housing agency is your best first call — they deal with this process regularly and know which providers work in your area.
English support varies widely by provider. On-base SoftBank stores can help with questions in English.
Speeds and reliability vary by area and provider. Ask neighbors in your housing complex what they use.
Some providers require a Japanese bank account to set up automatic billing — sort this out early.

title: Phone & SIM
provider: Options: keep US plan, add Japanese SIM, or switch carriers
summary: You have several options depending on your phone, budget, and how long you plan to stay. Most families use a combination — keeping a US number active while adding Japanese data.

contacts.label: SoftBank — Kadena Exchange
contacts.note: Inside the Kadena AB Exchange. English-speaking staff. Can set up plans, SIMs, and new devices.

contacts.label: SoftBank — Camp Foster Exchange
contacts.note: Inside the Camp Foster Exchange. English-speaking staff.

steps: Decide which option fits your situation — see Tips below for guidance.
If adding a Japanese SIM or plan, visit an on-base carrier store with your passport and residence card (在留カード).
If using eSIM, confirm your phone is unlocked and eSIM-compatible before visiting the store.
If keeping your US plan, contact your carrier before departure to confirm international coverage and pricing.
Test your setup as soon as possible — do not assume connectivity until you have confirmed it works on island.
tips: Option 1 — Keep your US plan: Major US carriers offer international plans that include Japan. This is the lowest-friction option if you want to keep your US number active with minimal changes. Check current pricing with your carrier before departure.

Option 2 — Add a Japanese SIM or eSIM: If your phone is unlocked, you can add a Japanese data SIM or eSIM while keeping your US plan active on your primary SIM. This gives you a local number and often cheaper data. Newer iPhones support dual SIM via eSIM — one US, one Japanese.

Option 3 — Switch to a Japanese carrier: For longer tours, switching fully to a Japanese carrier can significantly reduce costs. Rakuten Mobile offers unlimited data at low monthly rates. Docomo has the best island-wide coverage including rural and northern areas.
On-base SoftBank stores at Kadena and Foster have English-speaking staff and experience with SOFA-status customers — a good starting point regardless of which option you choose.
Make sure your US phone is unlocked before you leave — some carriers lock devices by default. Contact your carrier to unlock before departure.
Your residence card (在留カード) is required to sign up for any Japanese phone plan. Get this sorted at base admin as soon as you arrive.
Convenience store SIM cards (available at 7-Eleven and Family Mart) are a quick stopgap for data-only use while you sort out a longer-term plan.
If you buy a new phone on island, Japanese models may not support all US LTE bands — check compatibility before purchasing.
